--- Page 1 ---
1
Optimal Anchor Placement for Wireless Localization in Mixed
LOS and NLOS Scenarios
Gaurav Duggal, R. Michael Buehrer, Harpreet S. Dhillon, Jeffrey H. Reed
Abstract—We develop a unified Fisher-information framework
for localization in environments with both Line-of-Sight (LOS)
and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) paths, focusing on diffraction-
dominated NLOS propagation characteristic of Outdoor-to-
Indoor (O2I) signal propagation. The model couples anchor
geometry with a physically grounded path-loss law that is
continuous across the LOS/NLOS boundary and serves as an
optimization objective for our optimal anchor placement prob-
lem. As the first step, we analyze single-target anchor placement
and derive the classical A-, D-, and E-optimality criteria. Under a
specific path-loss assumption, these criteria collapse to a polygon-
closure condition in the complex plane: A-, D-, and E-optimal
designs coincide, yielding necessary and sufficient conditions for
optimal placement. Next, we extend the notion of optimal anchor
placement with respect to a single target to optimality over a
feasible region (multi-target setting) using a general formulation
that explicitly includes a realistic path loss model. This is achieved
by recasting the anchor placement as a combinatorial anchor-
selection problem with provable guarantees. Next we specify E-
and D-optimal objectives over multiple targets in a predefined
feasible target region and show that E-optimality straddles A-
optimality (within a constant factor), while D-optimality provides
looser bounds. These insights yield two practical algorithms—
both mixed-integer second-order cone programs (MISOCP) with
exact E-optimal and exact D-optimal objectives that produce
robust, region-wide designs under mixed LOS/NLOS conditions.
Index Terms—Wireless Localization, Fisher Information, non-
convex optimization, A/D/E-optimality, Optimal Anchor place-
ment, mixed LOS/NLOS, UAV
I. INTRODUCTION
Time-of-arrival (TOA)–based localization [1] forms the
foundation of many modern positioning systems, ranging from
global navigation satellite systems (GNSS) to indoor and
industrial networks that rely on ultra-wideband (UWB), Wi-
Fi, and cellular 5G/6G technologies. Its widespread adoption
stems from its ability to provide high-precision range esti-
mates using simple timing measurements between transmitters
and receivers. For this study we use anchors to refer to
entities whose positions are known a priori, such as base
stations, access points, or reference beacons, while targets
represent the agents whose positions are to be estimated. The
placement of these anchors has an impact on localization
accuracy and robustness. The localization error depends on
both the accuracy of the TOA measurements and the spatial
configuration of the anchors relative to the target. While TOA
measurement error is primarily determined by factors such as
signal bandwidth, signal strength, and noise level, the anchor
geometry dictates how these measurement errors propagate
into the estimate of the position. This geometric sensitivity is
commonly quantified by the Geometric Dilution of Precision
(GDOP), which captures the amplification of measurement
error due to anchor placement. However, GDOP does not ac-
count for signal coverage, which affects the measurement error
and is hence directly influenced by the anchor–target distances,
transmit power, and propagation conditions that govern the
SNR of each TOA link. Hence, localization accuracy can be
enhanced by deploying anchors in a manner that (a) improves
the SNR between each anchor and the target, and (b) ensures
a favorable spatial configuration that yields a good anchor
geometry (low GDOP).
Classical works on optimal anchor deployment have pre-
dominantly considered LOS scenarios, where localization ac-
curacy is determined by the anchor geometry and the SNR of
the direct propagation paths. Under these conditions, anchors
that are spatially well-separated and exhibit large angular
diversity with respect to the target position or the region of
interest [2]–[14] are regarded as geometrically advantageous,
as they help minimize position ambiguity and improve esti-
mation accuracy. Under this notion of geometric optimality,
and assuming distance independent path loss the theoretical
lower bound on the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for any
unbiased position estimator has also been derived [8], [13],
[15]. However, this notion of geometric optimality is valid
only under LOS propagation. In realistic mixed LOS/NLOS
environments, propagation effects such as reflection, diffrac-
tion [16], [17], and attenuation distort range measurements
and degrade SNR, and also invalidate the LOS-based ge-
ometric intuition for localization applications. Furthermore,
most existing formulations define optimality with respect to
a single fixed target whose position is to be estimated. These
approaches typically assume prior knowledge of the target
position, which is not very practical since in real scenarios the
target location is unknown and may vary across a wide region.
It is therefore essential to extend anchor deployment strategies
to the multi-target setting, enabling area-wide optimization and
ensuring consistent localization performance across the entire
region of interest. Preliminary efforts toward multi-target for-
mulations exist, notably [8], which analyzes a two-target case,
and [9], [10], which extends the framework to multiple targets.
However, these studies assume distance-independent path loss
and pure LOS conditions.
The optimality of anchor geometry is closely related to the
field of optimal experimental design [18], where scalar func-
tions of the Cram´er Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) matrix, derived
from the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM), are optimized to
achieve desired localization accuracy. There are three basic
approaches - A-optimality which minimizes the trace of the
CRLB matrix [2], [6]–[11], [13], [14], D-optimality [4], [6],
[7], [10] which minimizes the determinant of the CRLB matrix
and E-optimality [6], [7], [11] which minimizes the largest
arXiv:2604.00863v1  [eess.SP]  1 Apr 2026


--- Page 2 ---
2
eigenvalue of the CRLB matrix. Optimization frameworks that
optimize anchor geometry over all three criteria have been pro-
posed, in particular [6] shows all three criteria to be equivalent
for their formulation that assumes distance-independent path
loss, while [7] extends this to include distance dependent path
loss.
In terms of optimization techniques, most optimality formu-
lations are non-convex with respect to the anchor positions,
mainly due to the presence of distance dependent path loss.
Some tackle this non-convexity using heuristic approaches like
genetic algorithms [3]. Other approaches in literature directly
derive analytical solutions [5], [6], [15]. Other works propose
frameworks which tackle the nonconvex problem iteratively by
updating one block of variables at a time using convex surro-
gate functions that locally approximate and upper bound the
original nonconvex objective, ensuring a monotonic decrease
in the objective value at each iteration [9]–[11], [13]. In [12],
the authors employ a heuristics-based iterative approach where
anchors are added sequentially as a heuristic in the interest of
tractability. Although the authors [19] do not discuss anchor
optimality, they propose an interesting approach to solving
the problem of choosing a set of K sensor measurements,
from a set of M possible or potential sensor measurements,
that minimizes the error in estimating some parameter of the
measurement. This is in general a non convex continuous
problem which is reduced to a convex combinatorial sensor-
selection problem. We extend their ideas to formulate our own
optimization framework in this study.
In addition to theoretical interest, this problem has critical
relevance to indoor localization for next-generation emergency
response networks, such as firefighting operations, disaster
rescue, and active-shooter incidents [20]. In such scenarios,
precise localization of both first responders and at-risk indi-
viduals is vital for situational awareness and safety. Relying
on pre-installed localization infrastructure (e.g., Wi-Fi access
points or cellular base stations) is often impractical or unreli-
able during emergencies, when power failures, infrastructure
damage, or dense building materials can lead to degraded sig-
nal coverage indoors. To address this, mobile anchors—such as
UAVs — can be rapidly deployed around the affected building
to provide ad hoc localization support [21]. These systems
operate in Outdoor-to-Indoor (O2I) environments where signal
propagation is dominated by NLOS mechanisms, particularly
diffraction from window edges rather than direct LOS paths
[16]. In general, diffraction has been shown to emerge due
to bending around edges [17], [22], [23]. Designing optimal
anchor deployments in such environments thus requires jointly
accounting for anchor geometry, placement flexibility, and the
physical signal propagation mechanisms in NLOS scenarios.
Keeping in mind the limitations of prior art, our contributions
are as follows:
• A unified FIM framework for mixed LOS and NLOS
scenarios: We develop a unified FIM formulation for
TOA-based localization in multipath environments where
NLOS propagation arises from diffraction such as in O2I
scenarios. The proposed framework integrates a distance-
dependent path-loss model with a generalized diffraction
path-length formulation, both designed to remain well
behaved across LOS and NLOS conditions. This is par-
ticularly important since the anchor location can dictate
LOS or NLOS.
• Analysis of A, D, and E optimality objectives for the
single-target case: We analyze optimal anchor placement
according to A-,D- and E-optimality criteria for a single
target. Assuming an anchor-target distance-independent
path loss, we connect the optimal anchor placement
problem to a polygon closure problem in the complex
plane which helps us derive the conditions necessary to
achieving optimality. In this case, we show that if opti-
mality is met, all three optimality criteria coincide. Our
approach generalizes [6] to mixed LOS/NLOS settings
and provides structural insights into the problem.
• General optimization framework: Most importantly, we
extend the notion of optimal anchor placement beyond
one target, optimizing a common anchor configuration for
multiple targets located in a feasible region. Further, our
framework incorporates a distance-dependent path loss
for each anchor-target link for both LOS/NLOS scenarios.
To address the inherent nonconvexity we reformulate the
problem as a combinatorial anchor selection task via a
spatial discretization step.After this step, the optimization
variables change from continuous 3D anchor positions to
binary anchor-selection variables, each of which specifies
whether a particular candidate anchor location in 3D
space is selected simplifying our problem to a com-
binatorial problem with optimality guarantees. We use
a min–max method to generalize from a single target
to multi-target optimization over a region of interest.
Next, we show for the multi-target case, that both the E-
optimal and D-optimal objectives straddle the A-optimal
objective and hence minimizing D-,E-optimal criteria also
reduces the A-optimal criteria. We propose two algo-
rithmic formulations: two exact Mixed-Integer Second-
Order Cone Programs (MISOCPs) corresponding to the
E- and D-optimal criteria. Numerical results demonstrate
that this framework provides a theoretically grounded and
computationally scalable solution for region-wide anchor
optimization under mixed LOS/NLOS conditions.
II. MATHEMATICAL PRELIMINARIES
A. Fisher Information for Parameter Estimation
Let y ≜{y1, · · · , yM} be the observation vector, η ≜
{η1, · · · , ηK} be the parameter vector of the distribution of
y and the likelihood function be χ(y | η).
Definition 1. The Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) [24] Iη ∈
RK×K is
Iη = Ey|η

∇η ln χ(y | η) ∇η ln χ(y | η)⊤
,
(1)
B. FIM of the transformation of parameters
In many estimation problems, the parameters of interest are
functions of the parameters that appear directly in the likeli-
hood. For instance, one may estimate intermediate parameters
and then infer physical quantities through a known mapping. In


--- Page 3 ---
3
such cases, the Fisher Information can be propagated through
the parameter transformation as follows.
Definition 2. Let f : RK →RL denote a vector-valued func-
tion that maps the parameter vector η ∈RK to f(η) ∈RL.
The FIM of the transformed parameters, If(η) ∈RL×L, is
related to the FIM of the original parameters, Iη ∈RK×K,
through the Jacobian matrix J ∈RL×K as
If(η) = JT IηJ.
(2)
Here, J denotes the Jacobian matrix of f with columns
∂f
∂η1 , . . . , ∂f
∂ηK .
C. Equivalent FIM
In many applications, only a subset of parameters is of
interest, with the rest treated as nuisance variables. The
Equivalent FIM (EFIM) compactly captures the information
pertaining to the parameters of interest.
Definition 3. Consider the parameter vector η = [ηT
1 , ηT
2 ]T
with η ∈RN and η1 ∈RK, where K < N. The overall FIM
can be partitioned as
Iη =

Iη1,η1
IT
η2,η1
Iη2,η1
Iη2,η2

,
where Iη1,η1 ∈RK×K, Iη2,η1 ∈R(N−K)×K, and Iη2,η2 ∈
R(N−K)×(N−K). The EFIM of η1, obtained by treating η2 as
nuisance parameters, is given by the Schur complement [25]
as
I[e]
η1,η1 = Iη1,η1 −IT
η2,η1I−1
η2,η2Iη2,η1.
(3)
D. Cram´er–Rao Lower Bound on parameter estimation
The Cram´er–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) provides a fun-
damental lower bound on the covariance of any unbiased
estimator.
Definition 4. Let ˆη ∈RK be an unbiased estimator of the
parameter vector η ∈RK, derived from observations y. Then,
the error covariance matrix of ˆη satisfies the information
inequality:
Ey|η

(ˆη −η)(ˆη −η)⊤
⪰I−1
η
(4)
where Iη ∈RK×K is the FIM, defined element-wise as in
Definition 1 and I−1
η
is called the CRLB matrix.
III. MODEL FOR LOCALIZATION IN COMBINED
LOS/NLOS ENVIRONMENTS
As shown in Fig. 1, we study an O2I setting with K anchors
and N indoor targets, indexed by K ≜{1, . . . , K} and
N ≜{1, . . . , N}, respectively. Each anchor k ∈K transmits a
signal that is received by the nth target n ∈N. The K anchor
waveforms are mutually orthogonal, so there is no inter-
anchor interference at the receivers. From each anchor’s signal,
a target obtains one Time-Of-Flight (TOF) range estimate,
typically using the first-arrival path criterion [26]. Consistent
with [27], we assume the target’s floor level is known (e.g.,
via barometer data [28]), reducing the task to estimating the
target’s 2D position from the K TOF measurements i.e.,
ORIGIN
ANCHOR
BUILDING 
FLOOR
LOWER
DIFFRACTING EDGE
(Z=
)
MOUNTING 
HEIGHT OF RX
(Z=
)
Target
UPPER
DIFFRACTING EDGE
(Z=
)
ASSUMED
DIFFRACTING EDGE
(Z=
)
Fig. 1: In the O2I scenario, we have K anchors transmitting orthog-
onal signals which are received by the nth target inside the building.
The received signal includes several MPCs, from which the ranging
measurement corresponding to the diffraction path length AkQeNn
is extracted. A bandwidth of 200 MHz is assumed, ensuring that all
MPCs are resolvable.
mapping K ranges to a position estimate on the target building
floor. For the full 3D position estimation case, see our prior
work [16]. None of these works consider the optimal anchor
placement problem which is the focus of this study.
A. Received Signal Model
Assume that each anchor transmits a waveform sk(t), such
that it is mutually orthogonal between the anchors and has a
flat power spectral density (PSD) i.e.
|Sk(f)|2 =
(
1
B ,
for f ∈

−B
2 , B
2

0,
otherwise
.
(5)
The signal received at target n from anchor k during the
observation interval Tob can be expressed as
rn(t) =
L
X
l=1
hl sk(t −τl) + nn(t),
t ∈(0, Tob)
= h1sk(t −τ1)
|
{z
}
Diffraction MPC
+
L
X
l=2
hlsk(t −τl)
|
{z
}
Other MPCs
+nn(t)
(6)
This equation is to be interpreted as the received signal being a
superposition of delayed replicas of the transmitted waveform
from the kth anchor corresponding to L multipath components
(MPCs) represented by index set L ≜{1, . . . , L}. Each MPC
l ∈L is characterized by a delay τl, determined by its
propagation path length, and a path gain hl, which accounts
for attenuation along that path. MPCs generally arise from
electromagnetic interactions with the environment, including
specular reflections from planar surfaces (e.g., walls, floors,
and ceilings), transmissions through the building exterior and
inter-floor structures, and diffractions from window edges
present on each floor. At FR2 and FR3 in O2I NLOS settings,
the direct outdoor-to-indoor path is effectively absent due to
heavy attenuation from exterior walls, floors, and ceilings,
making diffraction the dominant mechanism; its MPCs can be
used for localization [16]. Among NLOS MPCs (reflections


--- Page 4 ---
4
and diffractions), diffraction paths are reliably isolated via the
first-arriving path (FAP). Frequency-dependent results in [16],
[26] show FR3 incurs lower diffraction loss than FR2, enabling
better localization performance.
Let l = 1 corresponding to the diffraction MPC between
the kth anchor and the target n, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The
TOF based ranging measurement is a noisy estimate of the
length of the diffraction path. In the next section, we begin by
introducing the diffraction path length formulation from [16].
In this study we obtain a simplified version that shows that it
is in fact a generalization of the conventional Euclidean path
model.
B. A Unified LOS/NLOS Path Model
Since the propagation delays for the various MPCs are
determined by their associated path lengths, we adopt a
simplified diffraction-based path length model, building upon
our earlier formulation [16], [27]. The full derivation is in
Appendix A. This model provides a unified treatment of
LOS and NLOS, with a smooth transition from Euclidean to
diffraction path length. It eliminates the need to label paths
as LOS or NLOS—the geometry determines the appropriate
regime.
In Fig. 1, two diffraction edges run parallel to the x-axis,
representing the upper and lower window edges on the target’s
floor. They lie at the intersections of the plane Y = 0 with
Z = zn + ∆(upper edge) and Z = zn −∆(lower edge).
Here 2∆is the vertical size of the windows. Let e ∈{l, u}
represent the lower and upper edge respectively. The location
of the diffraction point on the respective diffracting edge for
the kth anchor, is given by Q⟨e⟩
k,n = [x⟨e⟩
k,n, 0, 0]T , where
x⟨e⟩
k,n = xn+(xn−xk)
p
y2n+∆2
p
y2
k+(zn+se∆−zk)2+
p
y2n+∆2
(7)
e ∈{u, l}, su = +1, sl = −1.
Now, the diffraction path length pk,n between the kth anchor
at Ak = [xk, yk, zk]T and nth target at [xn, yn, zn]T can then
be expressed as
p⟨e⟩
k,n =
s
(xk−xn)2+
q
y2
k+(zn+se∆−zk)2+
p
y2n+∆2
2
(8)
e ∈{u, l}, su = +1, sl = −1.
We assume we can reliably isolate the shortest diffraction
path using the first arriving path principle [16]. If the anchor
is above the target, the upper-edge diffraction path is shortest;
if below, the lower-edge path is shortest. We can simplify
the model by assuming ∆= 0. Note, we made the same
assumption in our previous work [16] where we demonstrated
that ∆= 0 has minimal effect on the positioning accuracy.
Hence this leads to the following simplified path model
pk,n ≈
s
(xk −xn)2 +
q
y2
k + (zk −zn)2 + yn
2
.
(9)
Remark 1. When the anchor lies in the same horizontal plane
as the target, i.e., substitute zk = zn in (9), the expression
simplifies to
pk,n =
p
(xk −xn)2 + (|yk| + yn)2.
In this case, the additional diffraction-related detour vanishes,
and the path reduces to the direct Euclidean distance in the
2D plane Z = zn. This corresponds precisely to a LOS
path between the anchor and the target, hence presenting the
diffraction path length as a generalization of the Euclidean
path model.
C. Unified Path Loss for O2I mixed LOS/NLOS scenario
For O2I links, the path is LOS when the anchor is outside
the building at the same floor level as the target; if the anchor
is on a higher or lower floor, the path is NLOS and diffraction
dominated [20]. We therefore require a path loss model that
is well behaved in the LOS to NLOS transition region. We
adopt Knife Edge Diffraction (KED), a power domain model
that omits phase and polarization and remains well behaved
at the transition between LOS and NLOS, unlike GTD [29],
[30]. This provides a practical and computationally tractable
path loss model for mixed LOS and NLOS conditions.
Let the direct path ray from Ak to Nn intersect the front
wall of the building represented by the plane Y = 0 at a
point with height z0 above the origin. Denote the height of the
diffraction edge as zn. Since the diffraction path becomes LOS
when the anchor and target share z coordinates i.e., zk = zn
the path is LOS and the diffraction loss is set to zero; otherwise
it is NLOS and incurs an excess path loss. Define the intrusion
as
h ≜zk −zn
and the three dimensional distances to the edge diffraction
point
d1 = ∥Ak −Qe∥,
d2 = ∥Nn −Qe∥.
(10)
The Fresnel-Kirchhoff clearance parameter is obtained as
ν = h
s
2fc (d1 + d2)
c d1 d2
where c is the speed of light, fc is the center frequency. Now
the KED excess loss is given by [31]
Ld(ν) = 6.9 + 20 log10
p
(ν −0.1)2 + 1 + ν −0.1

dB.
(11)
Ld(ν) is inserted into the Friis free space path loss equation
as an excess path loss to form the received power and SNR,
providing a continuous power domain model across the LOS to
NLOS transition for the two edge O2I geometry. The received
SNR in dB is
SNRdB = P dBW
t
+ Gt + Gr −FSPL(R, fc) −Ld


is the free space
path loss, and Ld(ν) is the knife edge diffraction excess loss
(11). Here Pt is the transmit power in dBW. Gt and Gr are
the transmitter and receiver antenna gains in dBi. R = ∥Nn −
Ak∥is the anchor to target Euclidean distance. Ld(ν) is the
KED excess loss in dB defined in (11). NF is the receiver
noise figure in dB. K is the Boltzmann constant, B is the
receiver noise bandwidth in Hertz and T is the system noise
temperature in Kelvin.
D. TOF Ranging Estimation in Multipath scenarios
Our goal in this section is to obtain the CRLB on the TOF
based ranging estimate corresponding to the diffraction MPC
in the presence of other MPCs.
Lemma 1. Assuming the receive signal model in (6) our
estimation parameters are the delay of the L MPCs - τ ≜
[τ1, . . . , τL]T . Now the FIM Iτ ∈RL×L for estimating τ can
be written as
[Iτ]l1,l2 =

Here l1, l2 are pairwise indices of the MPCs and correspond
to the row and column indices of the FIM and δl1,l2 = τl1 −τl2
is called the path overlap coefficient.
Proof. Refer to Appendix B for proof.
It should be observed that the off-diagonal elements of
the FIM in (13) depend on the path overlap parameter
δl1,l2, l1, l2 ∈L, whereas the diagonal elements remain in-
dependent of it. For the diagonal terms, the ratio of the path
gain to the noise power can be interpreted as the SNR of the
lth path. We can thus express the lth diagonal entry as
[Iτ]l,l = 2π2B2SNRl
3
,
l ∈{1, . . . , L}.
Next, we partition the delay parameters as τ = [τ T
1 , τ T
2 ]T
where τ1 = τ1 is the delay of the diffraction path which we are
interested in and τ2 = [τ2, . . . , τL]T as the delays of the other
L −1 MPCs which are considered as nuisance parameters.
The corresponding FIM can be partitioned as
Iτ =

Iτ1,τ1
IT
τ2:L,τ1
Iτ2:L,τ1
Iτ2:L,τ2:L

where
Iτ1,τ1 ∈R, Iτ1,τ2:L ∈R1×L−1, Iτ2:L,τ2:L ∈R(L−1)×(L−1).
The EFIM I[e]
τ1 corresponding to the delay of the diffraction
path can now be expressed, using Definition 3, as
I[e]
τ1,τ1 = Iτ1,τ1 −I[l]
τ2,τL
where,
I[l]
τ2,τL = Iτ1,τ2:LI−1
τ2:L,τ2:LIT
τ1,τ2:L
and this represents the loss in information due to overlapping
paths. As the path overlap between MPCs l1, l2 approaches
zero i.e. ,δl1,l2 →∞, the CRLB (obtained using Definition 4)
for estimating the range of the diffraction path converges to
that of the single-path case. Thus when the path overlap is
zero, the variance of the ranging estimate for the diffraction
path between the kth anchor and the nth target satisfies the
CRLB
var(ˆrk,n) ≥
1
λk,n
,
λk,n = 2π2B2 SNRk,n
3c2
.
(14)
Here, λk,n is the ranging information weight. As the multipath
components become well separated, the information loss due
to path overlap vanishes. Empirically, evaluating the CRLB
versus path overlap at 50, 100, and 200 MHz shows that an
overlap larger than 1/B is typically sufficient to treat this loss
as negligible; i.e.,
lim
δl1,l2≫1
B
I[l]
τ2,τL = 0.
Remark 2. Thus, for a sufficiently large fixed bandwidth, the
accuracy of TOF-based ranging estimation depends solely on
the SNR of the diffraction path (12) and is unaffected by the
presence of the other MPCs.
IV. UNIFIED FIM FOR MIXED LOS/NLOS SCENARIOS
For the complete 3D localization, we assume that the
target’s z-coordinate is known (e.g., via barometric sensors
or FirstNet [28]) and focus on estimating its 2D position
on a given building floor using the TOA-based ranging
measurements. This assumption does not preclude full 3D
localization from TOF measurements, as demonstrated in [16],
but is adopted here to simplify the analysis of optimal anchor
placement. Since we define optimality with respect to the
Fisher information, we start by deriving the localization FIM
in two dimensions. With K anchors and TOA-based ranging
measurements, we seek the FIM for the target’s 2D location.
A. FIM for LOS/NLOS Range Measurements for K anchors
Let the K ranging measurements between a fixed target n
and each of the K anchors be collected in the vector rn ∈RK,
expressed as
rn = pn + nn,
(15)
Here, pn = [p1,n, . . . , pK,n]T is the vector of the true length
of the diffraction path from K anchors and the path length
for the kth anchor is given by (9). The range measurement
errors are modeled as Gaussian, owing to the assumed resolv-
ability of MPCs on each anchor–target link and the use of
orthogonal waveforms across the K anchors. Consequently,
the covariance matrix of nn is given by
Σn = E[nnnT
n] = diag

1
λ1 , . . . ,
1
λK

,
(16)
where E[·] denotes statistical expectation and λk, defined in
(14), is the reciprocal of the variance of the kth ranging


--- Page 6 ---
6
measurement. As the uncertainty in the ranging measurements
is fully captured by the noise vector nn, the FIM for estimating
the diffraction path from the K anchor measurements is the
inverse of the covariance matrix of the uncertainty in the
ranging measurements, given by
Irn = diag (λ1, . . . , λK) .
(17)
B. Formulation of the 2D Localization FIM in LOS/NLOS
Observe although the target is placed in 3D i.e. ,at dif-
ferent 2D locations across different building floors, since
we assume we know the building floor (using a barometer),
we only estimate the 2D position of each target inside the
building. Hence, the FIM for the nth target’s 2-D position,
I⟨2D⟩
n
∈R2×2, derived from the TOF-based ranging vector
rn = [r1,n, . . . , rK,n]⊤, is obtained by mapping the informa-
tion in the measurement space RK to the 2-D parameter space
R2; by Definition 2, it can be expressed as
I⟨2D⟩
n
= JT IrnJ,
(18)
where the Jacobian matrix J ∈RK×2 is defined as
J =


gT
1,n
...
gT
K,n

,
(19)
and Irn is the FIM of the ranging measurements from
(17), J is the Jacobian consisting of the information vector
corresponding to the kth anchor where gk,n = [ ∂pk,n
∂xn , ∂pk,n
∂yn ]T
expanded to
gk,n =
"
(xn −xk)
pk,n
, (
p
y2
k + (zk −zn)2 + yn)
pk,n
#T
.
(20)
Now to be able to estimate the 2D position Nn = [xn, yn]T
of the target, we require rank(J) = dim(Nn) = 2. Note that,
we can rewrite the 2D FIM as the weighted sum of the outer
product of the information vectors gk,n across K anchors as
I⟨2D⟩
n
=
K
X
k=1
λk,ngk,ngT
k,n.
(21)
From this formulation, we observe that each term gk,ngT
k,n
is a rank-one matrix whose column space is aligned with the
direction of gk,n. Consequently, the column space of the 2D
FIM coincides with the span of the information vectors gk,n.
Remark 3. For the FIM to be full rank, at least K = 2
anchors are required, with their information vectors gk,n being
linearly independent. Equivalently, a minimum of two anchors
is sufficient to estimate the unknown 2D position of a target
on a known building floor.
Observe, information vector gk,n is unit norm. Denote its x
and y components as [gk,n]x and [gk,n]y. Now, we can rewrite
[gk,n]x = cos ψk,n and [gk,n]y = sin ψk,n, where ψk,n
∈
(0, π) is the angle if gk,n was represented on the complex
plane i.e.
ψk,n = arctan 2
 p
y2
k + (zk −zn)2 + yn
xn −xk
!
.
(22)
Note that λk,n denotes the reciprocal of the ranging un-
certainty corresponding to the diffraction path, under the
assumption that the MPCs are resolvable as defined in (14).
Assuming fixed signal bandwidth, its value is only influenced
by the SNR associated with the diffraction/LOS path.
Sn ≜
K
X
k=1
λk,n,
un ≜
K
X
k=1
λk,n cos(2ψk,n),
(23)
vn ≜
K
X
k=1
λk,n sin(2ψk,n),
rn ≜
p
u2n + v2n =

K
X
k=1
λk,nej2ψk,n
.
These quantities are nonlinear functions of the anchor posi-
tions {Ak}k∈K. Here, Sn aggregates the ranging-information
weights and is negatively correlated to the received signal
strength at target n from each of the K anchors, while rn
captures the diffraction path geometry induced by the K
anchors.
Next, using these definitions, we present a reformulation of
the 2D FIM in compact matrix form as
I⟨2D⟩
n
= 1
2
Sn + un
vn
vn
Sn −un

.
(24)
As we will see in section V-C, this formulation has a strong
connection to the polygon closure problem. Assuming the FIM
is positive semi-definite i.e. ,I⟨2D⟩
n
⪰0, its eigenvalues can
be expressed as
µ± = Sn ± rn
2
.
(25)
Also the eigenvalues of the corresponding CRLB matrix are
µ−1
±
=
2
Sn ± rn
(26)
We use this formulation for further analysis.
V. ANCHOR OPTIMALITY FOR A SINGLE TARGET
In this section, we investigate anchor optimality for a single
target. For the purposes of this analysis, we temporarily
assume that the ranging-information weights λk,n are fixed
and do not depend on the anchor locations. This assumption,
also used in prior work [6], will be relaxed in our extension
to the multi-target case in the subsequent sections.
A. A-, D- and E-Optimality Objectives
For a FIM I ∈Rd×d, the CRLB on the error covariance is
I−1. The quadratic form e⊤I e is χ2
d-distributed [24], yielding
the (1 −α) confidence ellipsoid
E =

e ∈Rd : e⊤I e ≤χ2
d,1−α
	
.
(27)
Let {µi}d
i=1 be the eigenvalues of I−1. The ellipsoid’s princi-
pal axes align with the eigenvectors of I−1, and the semi-axis
lengths are
ai =
q
µi χ2
d,1−α,
i = 1, . . . , d.


--- Page 7 ---
7
Here, χ2
d,1−α is a scalar that scales the CRLB ellipsoid to
contain the estimation error with probability 1 −α according
to the confidence level α. In 2D, χ2
2,0.95 = 5.991 gives the
95% confidence ellipse.
Optimal design criteria minimize scalar performance mea-
sures derived from the CRLB matrix I−1, which represents the
fundamental error covariance in localization, by appropriately
selecting anchor positions.
a) A-optimality: The A-optimal design criterion seeks
to minimize the trace of the CRLB (i.e. ,the sum of the
variances). This corresponds to minimizing the sum of the
eigenvalues in (26), yielding
min ΦA = min tr

I⟨2D⟩
n
−1
(28)
= min (µ−1
+ + µ−1
−) = min
4Sn
S2n −r2n
.
This quantity represents the sum of the error variances along
orthogonal directions, and its square root is nown as the
Position Error Bound (PEB). The PEB provides a fundamental
lower bound on the root-mean-square error (RMSE) of any
unbiased localization estimator, thereby directly linking A-
optimality to minimizing localization error in the RMSE sense.
b) D-optimality: corresponds to minimizing the deter-
minant of the CRLB matrix or minimizing the volume of the
positioning error ellipsoid (2-D ellipse in our case). Equiv-
alently, this is the product of the eigenvalues of the CRLB
matrix in (26):
min ΦD = min det

I⟨2D⟩
n
−1
(29)
= min (µ−1
+ µ−1
−) = min
4
S2n −r2n
.
This criteria seeks to minimize the volume of the error
ellipsoid in (27). In localization terms, this criterion reduces
the overall volume of the confidence region.
c) E-optimality: seeks to minimize the maximum eigen-
value of the CRLB matrix in (26) and yields
min ΦE = min eigmax

I⟨2D⟩
n
−1
(30)
= min max{µ−1
+ , µ−1
−}
= min
2
Sn −rn
which corresponds to reducing the longest axis of the error
ellipsoid. This ensures that the localization error is not exces-
sively large in any direction, thereby improving robustness by
improving worst-case error orientations.
B. Optimization formulation for a single target with distance-
independent path loss
Let the position of the kth anchor be represented by Ak =
[xk, yk, zk]T
∈R3,
k ∈K. The set of optimal anchor
positions for a fixed target n is {A⋆
k}k∈K and can be obtained
as
Optimal Solution:
{A⋆
k}k∈K =
min
{Ak}k∈K
(31)
Optimal Objective:
Φ⋆
X = ΦX ({A⋆
k}k∈K) .
Here, ΦX denotes one of the A-, D-, or E-optimal objectives as
defined in (28), (29), and (30), respectively. The target location
is assumed to be fixed and known, so the 2D FIM depends
only on the K anchor positions {Ak}k∈K. the 2D FIM is
given by
I⟨2D⟩
n
({Ak}k∈K) =
K
X
k=1
Ik,n(Ak) ,
(32)
Ik,n(Ak) = λk,n(Ak) gk,n(Ak) gT
k,n(Ak) .
Here, Ik,n (Ak) is the Fisher Information contribution to-
wards target n from the kth anchor and is determined by both
the ranging information weight λk,n in (14) and the column
vector of the Jacobian of the diffraction path model gk,n
capturing the LOS/NLOS geometry.
Recall that under the assumptions of resolvability and fixed
signal bandwidth as observed in remark 2, λk,n depends solely
on the SNR between the anchor and the target, with the SNR
being the only term influenced by the anchor position. We
temporarily take λk,n to be anchor-position invariant (distance-
independent path loss) as in [6]. Hence, λk,n(Ak) = λk,n.
Theorem 1. Under the distance-independent path loss as-
sumption, the ranging information weights {λk,n}K
k=1 are
treated as constants. Consequently, the quantity Sn in (23),
signifying signal coverage, is fixed for the K anchors irre-
spective of their locations. For the FIM defined in (32), the
corresponding lower bounds on the A-, D-, and E-optimality
objectives for the nth target are
Φ⋆
A = 4
Sn
,
Φ⋆
D = 4
S2n
,
Φ⋆
E = 2
Sn
,
(33)
where Sn = PK
k=1 λk,n.
Proof. Starting from the A-, D-, and E-optimality objectives
in (28), (29), and (30), note that Sn is fixed and Sn > 0.
Each criterion ΦX is strictly increasing in r ∈[0, Sn). Hence,
the minimum of each objective is attained at the smallest
admissible value of rn, namely rn = 0. Substituting rn = 0
into the respective expressions yields the desired result.
Remark 4. Under the distance-independent path loss assump-
tion we have all three optimal objectives attain their optimum
at rn = 0. Consequently, any anchor geometry that optimizes
one of the A-, D-, or E-optimality criteria by achieving rn = 0
will also optimize the other two. Note there may be multiple
optimal solutions as explained in V-C.
C. Conditions for achieving Optimality: Polygon Closure
Observe that the condition rn = 0 directly implies un = 0
and vn = 0 defined in (23). Equivalently, in terms of ψk,n and
λk,n we get

K
X
k=1
λk,nej2ψk,n
 = 0.
(34)
Geometrically, the optimality condition can be interpreted in
the complex plane as the construction of a closed K-sided
polygon whose side lengths are given by {λ1,n, . . . , λK,n}.


--- Page 8 ---
8
61;1 = 1:5
62;1 = 2:0
63;1 = 2:3
64;1 = 2:5
X
-1
0
1
2
3
Y
-2
-1.5
-1
-0.5
0
0.5
1
1.5
2
2.5
(a) Closed Polygon construction
61;1 = 1:5, 2A1;1 = 138:1/
62;1 = 2:0, 2A2;1 = 314:7/
63;1 = 2:3, 2A3;1 = 17:2/
64;1 = 2:5, 2A4;1 = 186:0/
Real Part
-3
-2
-1
0
1
2
3
Imaginary Part
-3
-2
-1
0
1
2
3
(b) Complex Plane construction
Fig. 2: For a fixed target n = 1 with ranging-information weights
{λk,1}k∈{1,2,3,4}
=
{1.5, 2, 2.3, 2.5}, the generalized triangle
inequality (2) is satisfied, hence the optimal angles according to our
polygon closure argument are 2ψ1,1 = 138.2◦, 2ψ2,1 = 314.7◦,
2ψ3,1 = 17.2◦, and 2ψ4,1 = 186◦, achieving A-, D-, and E-
optimality simultaneously. The corresponding anchor positions can
be obtained by solving the transcendental equation in (22), similar to
the approach in [6].
As an example we have explained this pictorially in Fig. 2.
This raises the natural question: for a fixed set of {λk,n}
corresponding to K anchors, does there always exist a set
of orientations {2ψ1,n, . . . , 2ψK,n} such that the condition
in (34) is satisfied? Equivalently, can one always construct
a closed polygon in the complex plane with side lengths λk,n
and orientations 2ψk,n? This problem is closely related to
the generalized triangle inequality, which characterizes the
necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of such a
polygon.
Lemma 2. Let {λk,n}k∈K denote a set of nonnegative real
numbers, derived from the ranging uncertainties in (14), which
represent the magnitudes of K vectors in the complex plane C.
A necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of angles
{ψk}k∈K such that the vectors {λkej2ψk}k∈K form a closed
polygon is that the generalized polygon inequality holds:
λk,n ≤
X
i∈K\{k}
λi,n,
∀k ∈K.
Since our FIM formulation accounts for mixed LOS condi-
tions, the angles ψk,n are not the bearing angles as in [6], but
rather represent the angles between the information vectors. In
principle, if we can find angles 2ψk,n satisfying the optimality
conditions in (34), the anchor positions could be obtained by
solving (22), which is a transcendental equation and will have
several optimal anchor geometries as possible solutions. Thus,
clearly the optimal anchor locations are not unique.
VI. EXTENSION TO MULTIPLE-TARGETS
With single-target optimality established and linked to the
polygon-closure problem, we extend the framework to multiple
targets. We begin by reintroducing the distance dependent path
loss i.e. ,the scalar ranging information weights λk,n now vary
with the anchor positions rather than being fixed constants.
Observe that this makes our optimization problem in (31) in-
herently non-convex with respect to the optimization variables
- anchor positions {Ak}k∈K. To tackle this we reformulate
the optimization problem as a combinatorial problem making
the approach tractable. In this section we also introduce the
notion of anchor optimality over a feasible region by extending
our single-target optimization framework to include multiple-
targets spread over the region of interest.
A. Anchor Discretization and Combinatorial Reformulation
Instead of trying to optimally place K anchors outside the
building, we sample the 3D space outside the building into
M points represented by the index set M ≜{1, · · · , M}.
Each point m ∈M represents a candidate anchor location
and our optimization problem can be reformulated as selecting
K anchors from the candidate anchor set M such that our
optimality criteria is met. The anchor selection can modeled
using binary variables xm ∈{0, 1}, where xm = 1 indicates
that candidate anchor m ∈M is chosen. For each target n ∈
N and candidate anchor location indexed by m ∈M, we
can reformulate the coverage and the geometry term defined
in (24) for the discretized scenario as follows
Sn({xm}m∈M) ≜
X
m∈M
λm,nxm,
(35)
rn({xm}m∈M) ≜
p
u2n + v2n =

X
m∈M
λm,nej2ψm,nxm
.
Observe in the above reformulation, λm,n and ψm,n are now
fixed scalars that represents the ranging information weight
between the nth target and mth candidate anchor location and
our optimization variables are the binary variables {xm}m∈M.
Further our coverage term Sn and geometry term rn are
affine and quadratic with respect to the optimization variables.
Further Sn and rn are affine and quadratic with respect to the
optimization variables.
With the coverage term and geometry terms defined in (35),
we can now derive the 2D FIM for the discretized setting using
(24) and the eigenvalues of the CRLB matrix in (26) to yield
the three optimality objectives for each target n ∈N as
Φ⋆
A,n =
min
{xm}m∈M
4Sn
S2n −r2n
=
max
{xm}m∈M
S2
n −r2
n
4Sn
,
(36)
Φ⋆
D,n =
min
{xm}m∈M
4
S2n −r2n
=
max
{xm}m∈M
S2
n −r2
n
4
,
(37)
Φ⋆
E,n =
min
{xm}m∈M
2
Sn −rn
=
max
{xm}m∈M
Sn −rn
2
(38)
Note that under the continuous relaxation xm ∈[0, 1], the E-
opt objective Sn −rn is concave, and the D-opt formulation
is implemented by maximizing a quantity proportional to
p
det(In) =
1
2
p
S2n −r2n, which is concave over In ⪰0.
This structure is well suited to commercial MISOCP solvers
using branch-and-bound; see Section VI-E for details.
Remark 5. We have the target locations fixed and candidate
anchors sampled with a spatial resolution d from the feasible
region (outside the building). We precompute λm,n and ψm,n
for all pairs (m, n) using (14) and (22). These fixed values


--- Page 9 ---
9
form an anchor–target dictionary. This is fed as an input to
the optimizer (solver) which then selects anchors {xm}m∈M
that optimize the objective within the feasible region. In
essence, the dictionary captures geometry and signal coverage
information, while the optimizer selects the best anchors.
Discretizing the feasible region thus converts the continuous
placement task into a combinatorial anchor selection problem,
in which the objective is to select K anchors from M
candidates to optimize the chosen criterion across all N fixed
indoor targets.
B. Multi Target Objective: Global vs Min–Max Formulations
After the discretization step there are two ways to extend our
single target objectives to multi-target. Recall, for a network of
N targets, each target n is associated with a local 2D position
estimation FIM denoted by
I⟨2D⟩
n
␀{xm}m∈M

∈S2×2
++ ,
determined by the anchor configuration {xm}m∈M. The over-
all FIM for estimating the 2D position for all targets is block-
diagonal and is given by
Iglob
␀{xm}m∈M

= blkdiag

I⟨2D⟩
1
, . . . , I⟨2D⟩
N

,
(39)
whose inverse and determinant factor across targets as
I−1
glob = blkdiag
␀(I⟨2D⟩
1
)−1, . . . , (I⟨2D⟩
N
)−1
and
det Iglob =
N
Y
n=1
det I⟨2D⟩
n
.
Accordingly, the classical global optimal design criteria ag-
gregate information across all targets:
A-opt:
min
{xm}m∈M
Φglob
A
= tr
␀I−1
glob

=
N
X
n=1
tr
␀(I⟨2D⟩
n
)−1
,
(40)
D-opt:
min
{xm}m∈M
Φglob
D
= det(I−1
glob)
(41)
=
N
Y
n=1
det((I⟨2D⟩
n
)−1),
E-opt:
min
{xm}m∈M
Φglob
E
= eigmax
␀(Iglob)−1
(42)
= max
n
eigmax
␀(I⟨2D⟩
n
)−1
.
While the global A- and D-objectives minimize the average
or aggregate estimation error across all targets, they do not
explicitly control the weakest-performing target. To ensure
fairness and robustness, we use a min–max formulation in-
stead. This optimizes the worst-case per-target objective and
is given by
A-opt (min–max):
min
{xm}m∈M
max
n
tr
␀(I⟨2D⟩
n
)−1
,
(43)
D-opt (min–max):
min
{xm}m∈M
max
n det
␀(I⟨2D⟩
n
)−1
,
(44)
E-opt (min–max):
min
{xm}m∈M
max
n
eigmax
␀(I⟨2D⟩
n
)−1
,
(45)
where the last expression (45) coincides exactly with the
global E-optimal objective (42) because the smallest eigen-
value of the block-diagonal FIM equals the minimum of
the smallest eigenvalues of its constituent blocks. Thus, E-
optimality naturally enforces fairness across targets, whereas
A- and D-optimality require explicit min–max reformulations
to prevent sacrificing accuracy at poorly conditioned targets
hence we follow the minimizing the maximum optimality
objective across all targets for the rest of this paper.
C. Relationship among A-, D-, and E-optimal objectives
Recall that the per-target D-opt objective in (37) and E-opt
objective in (38) were concave in the continuous relaxation of
the optimization variables. In this section, we show both the
E-optimal and D-optimal objectives control the A-optimal ob-
jective and this extends to the multi-node setting as well. The
following proposition formalizes these relationships among the
A-, D- and E-optimal objectives.
Proposition 1 (Relationship between A-, D- and E-optimality
objectives). For each target n ∈N, we have
(i) (E straddles A) For all n,
ΦE,n ≤ΦA,n ≤2 ΦE,n.
(46)
(ii) (D controls A) For all n,
ΦA,n = Sn ΦD,n.
(47)
In particular, if Sn is fixed (or bounded) across feasible
designs, then any design that decreases ΦD,n also
decreases ΦA,n. Moreover, if Smin ≤Sn ≤Smax for
all feasible designs, then
Smin ΦD,n ≤ΦA,n ≤Smax ΦD,n.
(48)
Proof. For (i), the A-opt per-target objective can be rewritten
as
ΦA,n =
4Sn
S2n −r2n
=
2
Sn −rn
+
2
Sn + rn
= ΦE,n +
2
Sn + rn
,
and since 0 ≤rn ≤Sn implies Sn −rn ≤Sn + rn, we get
2
Sn + rn
≤
2
Sn −rn
= ΦE,n.
Therefore,
ΦE,n ≤ΦA,n ≤ΦE,n + ΦE,n = 2 ΦE,n,
which proves (46).
For (ii), This follows directly from the definitions of A- and
D-optimality in (36) and (37).
Remark 6 (Extension to the multi-target case). Proposition 1
extends directly to the multi-target setting using the min–max
formulation, by taking the maximum over all targets n ∈N.
Now we bring all of these ideas together in two algorithms
with exact D- and E-optimal objectives.


--- Page 10 ---
10
1) Algorithm 1: E-Opt MISOCP: We seek to select ex-
actly K anchors such that the E-optimal objective in (38) is
maximized for the worst performing target. In other words
the E-optimal objective aims to maximize the the difference
between the polygon perimeter Sn and the residual rn. The
selected anchors describe, for each target n, a K-sided polygon
in the information domain. The resulting optimization problem
can be formulated as
E-Opt-MISOCP
max
{xm}m∈M
γ
(49)
s.t.
M
X
m=1
xm = K,
xm ∈{0, 1},
(50)
Sn =
M
X
m=1
λm,nxm,
pn =
M
X
m=1
λm,n cos
␀2ψm,n

xm,
qn =
M
X
m=1
λm,n sin
␀2ψm,n

xm,
∀n ∈N,
(51)
r2
n ≥p2
n + q2
n,
∀n ∈N,
(52)
γ ≤Sn −rn,
∀n ∈N,
(53)
rn ≥0, γ ≥0,
∀n ∈N.
(54)
The objective is to maximize a common scalar number
γ such that it lower bounds the E-optimal criteria for all
targets n ∈N. This is enabled through constraint (53).
Our optimization variables are the indicator variables xm ∈
{0, 1}, m ∈M which represent which anchors are selected.
Constraint (50) is the cardinality constraint that ensures exactly
K anchors are selected out of a possible M. Now, for each
target n ∈N we calculate a perimeter defined in (51) and
a residual side length defined in (52). Next, we ensure our
objective γ lower bounds the E-optimality criteria for target
n from (38). Finally, the constraint (54) ensures nonnegativity
of γ and the residual rn term. Notice that the optimization
problem features a linear objective and linear constraints,
with the exception of (52), which imposes a convex quadratic
constraint. This structure classifies the problem as a Second-
Order Cone Program (SOCP). Furthermore, since the anchor
selection variables xm are restricted to be binary, the resulting
formulation is a Mixed-Integer Second-Order Cone Program
(MISOCP).
D. D-optimal solution
a) Algorithm3: D-Opt MISOCP: Observe that the D-
optimality criterion in (37), it follows that maximizing τ is
equivalent to maximizing the worst-case determinant of the
FIM per target det(In) = 1
4(S2
n −r2
n), across all targets, i.e.
det(In)
≥
τ 2
4 ,
∀n ∈N.
Thus the D-optimal algorithm can be formulated as
D-Opt-MISOCP
max
{xm}m∈M
τ
s.t.
M
X
m=1
xm = K,
xm ∈{0, 1},
(55)
Sn =
M
X
m=1
λm,nxm,
pn =
M
X
m=1
λm,n cos
␀2ψm,n

xm,
qn =
M
X
m=1
λm,n sin
␀2ψm,n

xm,
∀n ∈N,
(56)
r2
n ≥p2
n + q2
n,
∀n ∈N,
(57)
an = Sn −rn,
bn = Sn + rn,
an, bn ≥0, ∀n ∈N,
(58)
σ2
n ≤2anbn,
σn ≥0, ∀n ∈N,
(59)
√
2 τ ≤σn,
∀n ∈N,
(60)
τ ≥0, rn ≥0.
(61)
The objective is to maximize a scalar τ by flipping indicator
variables xm ∈{0, 1}, m ∈M. Constraint (55) enforces
the cardinality condition, ensuring that exactly K anchors are
selected out of the available M. For each target n ∈N,
the perimeter Sn and residual side length rn are defined in
(56)–(57). Auxiliary variables an, bn are introduced in (58)
to symmetrize the problem, while the rotated second-order
cone (SOC) constraint (59) ensures that σn ≤
√
2
p
S2n −r2n.
Constraint (60) then guarantees that the global objective
√
2τ
lower bounds all σn values across the targets (∀n ∈N).
The problem therefore has a linear objective and linear con-
straints, except for (57) and (59), which are convex quadratic
constraints. This places the problem in the class of SOCP.
Furthermore, because the anchor selection variables xm are
binary, the final formulation is a MISOCP.
E. Branch-and-Bound for MISOCP
Mixed-integer formulations such as MISOCP are typically
solved via branch-and-bound [32], [33]. The method branches
on binary selection variables ({xm}m∈M in our case) to build
a search tree; at each node, the binary constraints are relaxed
to continuous values which yields a convex subproblem—
an SOCP for MISOCP—whose optimal value provides a
valid lower bound. We refer to the incumbent as the best
feasible solution found so far. These bounds drive pruning:
any node whose relaxation cannot outperform the incumbent
is discarded, so only promising regions of the search space
are explored.
Optimality guarantees. For the current discretized and
sampled anchor-target dictionary, branch and bound certifies
epsilon optimality once the gap between the incumbent and the
best relaxation bound is at or below a user specified tolerance,
assuming exact node relaxations and correct pruning or ex-
haustive enumeration [34]. In this setting, the certificates are:
(i) Feasibility the incumbent satisfies all constraints including
integrality; (ii) epsilon optimality certificate the incumbent
objective matches or is within ϵ of the global bound from the
relaxations, that is gap ≜(bound −incumbent) ≤ϵ with


--- Page 11 ---
11
the usual sign convention for minimization or maximization;
and (iii) Infeasibility certificate the search can prove that no
feasible solution exists within the discretized sample region.
F. Interpretation as a Polygon Closure problem
In the multi-target case, achieving optimality is no longer
equivalent to closing the polygon for each target individually.
For instance, under the E-optimal objective, maximizing the
denominator Sn −rn requires simultaneously maximizing the
coverage term Sn and minimizing the residual rn. However,
since all targets share the same set of anchors, it may not
be possible to close the polygon for every target. Moreover,
the polygon closure itself may not be achievable, as the
generalized triangle inequality in Lemma 2 may not hold for
anchors that yield the highest signal coverage. Observe in
Fig. 3, the optimizer balances the tradeoff between maximiz-
ing overall coverage Sn and achieving favorable geometric
configurations rn across all targets. Geometrically, for each
target n ∈N, Sn represents the perimeter of a polygon with
sides {λm,n : xm = 1} oriented by {2ψm,n : xm = 1} in the
complex plane, while rn denotes the residual, i.e., the length
of the polygon’s unclosed side.
 Target:1, PEB:0.36m  Target:2, PEB:1.03m  Target:3, PEB:0.42m  Target:4, PEB:1.21m 
 Target:5, PEB:0.32m  Target:6, PEB:0.87m  Target:7, PEB:0.31m  Target:8, PEB:0.91m 
 Target:9, PEB:0.36m  Target:10, PEB:1.04m  Target:11, PEB:0.43m  Target:12, PEB:1.15m 
Fig. 3: Polygon closure for 12 targets spread across 3 floors of a
building for the optimal solution obtained using E-opt-MISOCP. The
optimization algorithm attempts to simultaneously close the polygon
as well as reduce their perimeters by selecting the best 4 anchors so
as to optimize the E-opt objective for the worst performing target.
VII. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
A. Performance Metrics
Let {xm}⋆
m∈N denote the solution to the optimization
problem. Define the worst-performing target nworst ∈N. For
this target, compute Sn and rn via (35), and the corresponding
CRLB matrix as
Σ⋆
nworst :=

I⟨2D⟩
nworst
−1
from (24). We report three scalar measures of localization
accuracy:
PEB⋆
nworst =
q
tr
␀Σ⋆nworst

,
(62)
ρ⋆
nworst =
r
5.991

det
␀Σ⋆nworst

,
(63)

␀Σ⋆nworst

.
(64)
TABLE I: System and Geometric Simulation Parameters
Parameter

Here, PEBn⋆(PEB) gives the RMS position error from
the CRLB via
p
tr(Σ⋆n); ρn⋆(Confidence Ellipsoid Radius
(CER)) is the radius of a 95% equal-area circle for the error
ellipse, using χ2
2,0.95 = 5.991; and σmax,n⋆(Maximum Axis
Deviation (MAD)) is the worst-direction standard deviation
error obtained from the largest eigenvalue of Σ⋆
n. All three
have units of length and are scaled versions of the A-,D- and
E-optimality criteria respectively.
B. Simulation Methodology
We assess performance via a Monte Carlo study with 300
trials. Target locations are fixed. In each trial t, we first
form a uniform 3D grid of candidate anchors {Am}m∈M
with spacing d meters along each of the x, y, z axes. We
then apply a Cranley–Patterson random shift [35] by drawing
a vector on every trial t such that u(t) = [ux, uy, uz]T
with ui
i.i.d.
∼U([−d
2, d
2]), shifting every grid point by u(t),
and wrapping any out-of-domain points to within the fea-
sible region. Using this candidate set, we precompute the
anchor–target dictionary parameters {λm,n, ψm,n}, solve for
the anchor selections {x(t)
m }m∈M, and compute the accuracy
metrics. We report boxplots of each metric (62),(63),(64) for
the worst-performing target across trials. The other simulation
parameters are given in Table I.
C. Numerical comparison of the algorithms
As a baseline, we use a random minimax heuristic: in
each of 10, 000 10, 000 trials we select K anchors uni-
formly at random, evaluate the worst-case A-,D- and E-
optimal objectives over all targets, and retain the anchor set
with the best (smallest) worst-case metric. We refer to these
baselines as RandMinimax-A-opt, RandMinimax-D-opt, and
RandMinimax-E-opt. Next, we vary the size of our anchor-
target dictionary by evaluating performance over different
sampling distances d. Observe, in Fig. 4b the D-opt-MISOCP
algorithm consistently achieves the lowest CER across all
d values. This is because CER directly represents the D-
optimal objective, validating its exactness and efficiency in
maximizing the determinant of the FIM. Note that the MAD


--- Page 12 ---
12
PEB (m)
1.5
2
2.5
3
3.5
4
4.5
d = 5m
d = 4m
d = 3m
RandMinimax-A-opt
E-opt-MISOCP
D-opt-MISOCP
(a) PEB plot
CER (m)
1.6
1.8
2
2.2
2.4
2.6
2.8
3
3.2
d = 5m
d = 4m
d = 3m
RandMinimax-D-opt
E-opt-MISOCP
D-opt-MISOCP
(b) CER plot
MAD (m)
1.5
2
2.5
3
3.5
4
4.5
d = 5m
d = 4m
d = 3m
RandMinimax-E-opt
E-opt-MISOCP
D-opt-MISOCP
(c) MAD plot
Fig. 4: Localization performance of the E-opt-MISOCP, and D-opt-MISOCP formulations with K = 4 anchors versus sampling distance
d. Each boxplot summarizes variability across Monte Carlo trials: the horizontal line marks the median; the box spans the 25%–75%
Inter-Quartile-Range (IQR) range; whiskers extend to 1.5× IQR; and dots indicate outliers beyond the whiskers. Hence, shorter boxes and
whiskers indicate tighter dispersion (greater robustness), while a lower median indicates better typical performance. For larger d, the solver
attains optimality within the 70 s time limit; for denser dictionaries (d < 5 m), solutions are high quality but not provably optimal within
the time limit. Among the objectives, D-opt-MISOCP achieves the lowest CER, E-opt-MISOCP attains the smallest MAD, and both also
reduce PEB, consistent with the theoretical relationships among A-, D-, and E-opt criteria in Proposition 1. The baseline performance is set
by the random search over all three optimality criteria.
metric is precisely the objective optimized by the E-opt-
MISOCP formulation. Consequently, as shown in Fig. 4c, the
E-opt-MISOCP solution attains the lowest MAD among all
algorithms for every sampling distance. In contrast, the PEB
is not the exact objective for any of the proposed algorithms.
Nevertheless, Proposition 1 shows that the E-optimal criterion
bounds the A-optimal objective within a factor of two; thus,
minimizing E-opt provably decreases the PEB and serves as
a certified surrogate for improving A-opt performance. Like-
wise, the D-optimal formulation reduces the PEB indirectly
through its dependence on the scalar coverage term Sn.
Increasing the sampling distance d refines the granularity
of the feasible anchor dictionary, allowing the solver to ex-
plore better configurations. However, larger dictionaries also
increase computational complexity, and within the fixed time
limit, the solvers cannot always reach proven optimality. As
shown in Fig. 5, D-opt-MISOCP and E-opt-MISOCP converge
to provable optimality within the scope of the current dictio-
nary in the 70-second limit for d = 5 m for a few trials. At finer
sampling resolutions, although optimality is not certified, the
incumbent solutions consistently yield improved performance
across all three metrics, as illustrated in Fig. 4.
This trade-off between performance and computational
tractability highlights the practical balance achieved by the
proposed MISOCP formulations.
CONCLUSION
We presented a unified Fisher–information-based framework
for optimal anchor placement in mixed LOS/NLOS O2I envi-
ronments where NLOS propagation is diffraction-dominated.
The framework couples a generalized diffraction path-length
model that gracefully reduces to the Euclidean distance in LOS
with a continuous KED-based path-loss/SNR model, enabling
a single treatment across propagation regimes. For the single-
target case (with distance-independent path loss), we showed
that A-, D-, and E-optimal designs coincide and admit a
polygon-closure interpretation, yielding clear optimality condi-
tions. We then extended the framework to the multi-target case
1
2
3
4
5
6
7
8
9
Computation Time per Trial (s)
0
10
20
30
40
50
60
70
80
d = 5
d = 4
d = 3
RandMinimax (A/D/E)
E-opt-MISOCP
D-opt-MISOCP
Fig. 5: Finer sampling (smaller d) for the anchor-target dictionary
in remark 5 yields more candidate anchors and longer solve times; a
70 s per-trial time limit is imposed. If the solver does not terminate
before this limit, it returns the best incumbent solution found up to
that point.
via a discretization step and formulating min–max objectives,
leading to tractable optimization programs with guarantees.
The multi-target case can also be used to optimize over a
feasible region. Numerical results show consistent accuracy
gains and robust layouts across LOS/NLOS environments.
APPENDIX
A. Diffraction Path Length Derivation
Observe from Fig. 1, we have the nth target at Nn =
[xn, yn, zn]T , anchor indexed by k at Ak = [xk, yk, zk]T
and the diffracting edge e ∈{u, l}. Define the length of the
perpendiculars from the target to the diffraction edge e as r
and from the anchor to the diffraction edge as r⊥,e, we have
r =
p
y2n + ∆2,
r⊥,e =
q
y2
k + (zn −zk + se∆)2,
where e ∈{u, l}, su = 1, sl = −1. The diffraction path length
pk,n between the target and the kth anchor can be written as
a sum of two distances AkQ + QNn and expanded to
p⟨e⟩
k,n =
q
(x⟨e⟩
k,n −xn)2 + r2 +
q
(x⟨e⟩
k,n −xk)2 + r2
⊥,e. (65)


--- Page 13 ---
13
Using Fermat’s Principle of Least time (or equivalently the
Diffraction law) [16] we set the first derivative of the path
length with respect to the x-coordinate x⟨e⟩
k,n of the diffraction
point to zero i.e.
δp⟨e⟩
k,n
δx⟨e⟩
k,n = 0. We obtain the following expres-
sion
(x⟨e⟩
k,n −xn)2
(x⟨e⟩
k,n −xn)2 + r2 =
(x⟨e⟩
k,n −xk)2
(x⟨e⟩
k,n −x)2 + r2
⊥,e
(66)
After some algebraic manipulations we get two solutions
x⟨e⟩
k,n = rxk + r⊥,exn
r + r⊥,e
,
x⟨e⟩
k,n = rxk −r⊥,exn
r −r⊥,e
The first solution ensures x⟨e⟩
k,n lies between x and xi whereas
the second solution lies outside this interval and can be
ignored. This is a direct consequence of the Fermat’s principle
of least time since the first solution provides a smaller path
length. Next, we can substitute the first solution in (65) to
obtain the final path length result in (8).
B. FIM of the Diffraction Path Delay
Using the definition of the received signal in (6) and
assuming Gaussian noise with power N0, the log-likelihood
function of the received signal (6) can be written as
log χ(r(t)|η) = −1

we obtain l1, l2 ∈L entry of the FIM as
